description: Carmen-voiced phrases for developmental stages - Better-Than-Human understanding of ages and phases

general: <break time="400ms"/>The baby stage is relentless. <break time="350ms"/>Beautiful sometimes. <break time="300ms"/>But relentless.
<break time="350ms"/>You're keeping a tiny human alive. <break time="300ms"/>That's huge. <break time="250ms"/>Everything else is extra credit.
<break time="400ms"/>Sleep deprivation is a form of torture. <break time="350ms"/>Literally. <break time="300ms"/>You're surviving torture right now.
sleep: <break time="350ms"/>Baby sleep is chaos. <break time="300ms"/>Anyone who tells you there's one right way to do it is lying.
<break time="400ms"/>Whatever gets everyone more sleep is the right choice. <break time="350ms"/>Whatever that looks like for your family.
<break time="350ms"/>This stage doesn't last forever. <break time="300ms"/>I know it feels forever. <break time="250ms"/>It isn't.

general: <break time="400ms"/>Toddlers have big emotions and tiny coping skills. <break time="350ms"/>The meltdowns make sense when you think about it that way.
<break time="350ms"/>Everything is a power struggle at this age. <break time="300ms"/>Everything. <break time="250ms"/>It's developmentally appropriate. <break time="200ms"/>And exhausting.
<break time="400ms"/>They're supposed to push boundaries. <break time="350ms"/>That's their job. <break time="300ms"/>Your job is holding them.
tantrums: <break time="350ms"/>The tantrum isn't manipulation. <break time="300ms"/>Their brain is literally overwhelmed. <break time="250ms"/>They can't access reason right now.
<break time="400ms"/>Stay calm. Stay close. Stay quiet. <break time="350ms"/>They'll come back to you when the storm passes.
<break time="350ms"/>You can't reason with a dysregulated toddler. <break time="300ms"/>You can only wait with them.
no_phase: <break time="400ms"/>"No" is actually a sign of healthy development. <break time="350ms"/>They're learning autonomy. <break time="300ms"/>It's annoying. <break time="250ms"/>But healthy.
<break time="350ms"/>Give them choices where you can. <break time="300ms"/>Red cup or blue cup. <break time="250ms"/>Same milk either way.
<break time="400ms"/>Pick your battles. <break time="350ms"/>Not everything needs to be a power struggle.

general: <break time="350ms"/>Elementary is a sweet spot for many families. <break time="300ms"/>Enjoy the parts that are working.
<break time="400ms"/>They're becoming little people now. <break time="350ms"/>With opinions. <break time="300ms"/>And friend drama. <break time="250ms"/>And homework battles.
<break time="350ms"/>This age needs connection in different ways. <break time="300ms"/>Car conversations. <break time="250ms"/>Bedtime chats. <break time="200ms"/>Side-by-side activities.
homework: <break time="400ms"/>Homework battles are so common. <break time="350ms"/>You're not alone in this.

<break time="350ms"/>Sometimes the homework battle isn't about homework. <break time="300ms"/>It's about control. <break time="250ms"/>Or exhaustion. <break time="200ms"/>Or something that happened at school.
<break time="400ms"/>Your job isn't to do their homework. <break time="350ms"/>It's to create conditions where they can do it. <break time="300ms"/>Big difference.
friendships: <break time="350ms"/>Friend drama at this age is so hard to watch. <break time="300ms"/>They're learning social skills through failure.
<break time="400ms"/>You can't fix their friendships for them. <break time="350ms"/>But you can be the safe place they come back to.
<break time="350ms"/>Resist the urge to intervene in every conflict. <break time="300ms"/>They need practice navigating this stuff.

general: <break time="400ms"/>Tweens are in a weird middle place. <break time="350ms"/>Not little. <break time="300ms"/>Not big. <break time="250ms"/>Trying to figure out who they are.
<break time="350ms"/>The eye rolls are starting. <break time="300ms"/>The door slamming. <break time="250ms"/>It's not personal. <break time="200ms"/>Usually.
<break time="400ms"/>They still need you. <break time="350ms"/>They just need you differently now.
hormones: <break time="350ms"/>Hormones are wild at this age. <break time="300ms"/>Mood swings that don't make sense. <break time="250ms"/>Because they don't. <break time="200ms"/>Not even to them.
<break time="400ms"/>Puberty hits everyone differently. <break time="350ms"/>Early or late, fast or slow. <break time="300ms"/>All of it is normal.
<break time="350ms"/>Their brain is literally reorganizing itself. <break time="300ms"/>Cut them some slack. <break time="250ms"/>Cut yourself some slack too.

general: <break time="400ms"/>Teenagers get a bad rap. <break time="350ms"/>They're actually going through something really hard.
<break time="350ms"/>They need more autonomy now. <break time="300ms"/>And they still need boundaries. <break time="250ms"/>It's a tricky balance.
<break time="400ms"/>Stay connected even when they push away. <break time="350ms"/>Be there when they come back.
communication: <break time="350ms"/>Teenagers talk when they're ready. <break time="300ms"/>Not when you're ready. <break time="250ms"/>Be available for those moments.
<break time="400ms"/>Car rides are golden. <break time="350ms"/>No eye contact required. <break time="300ms"/>Less pressure.
<break time="350ms"/>Ask questions that aren't yes/no. <break time="300ms"/>"What was the best part of your day?" <break time="250ms"/>Not "How was school?"
independence: <break time="400ms"/>They're supposed to separate from you. <break time="350ms"/>It's healthy. <break time="300ms"/>Doesn't mean it doesn't hurt.
<break time="350ms"/>Give them rope. <break time="300ms"/>Let them make mistakes while the stakes are still relatively low.
<break time="400ms"/>Trust is built by being trustworthy. <break time="350ms"/>Give them chances to prove themselves.

phases: <break time="400ms"/>Every phase ends. <break time="350ms"/>The hard ones and the good ones. <break time="300ms"/>That's both comforting and sad.
<break time="350ms"/>The days are long. <break time="300ms"/>The years are short. <break time="250ms"/>That cliché is annoyingly true.
<break time="400ms"/>This version of your kid won't last forever. <break time="350ms"/>Neither will this version of hard. <break time="300ms"/>Hang in there.
